data.title: After Two Detrimental Earnings Events, Could AECOM Be Third Time Lucky?
data.description:
Introduction

AECOM (ACM) is a Dallas-based professional services business that is seen as a reliable authority in infrastructure consulting and advisory work (rendered mainly for global “public” clients that account for almost three-fourths of total business) in key end markets such as water, transportation, and facilities.

ACM’s underlying strength in offering fee-based knowledge-based services has been validated by ENR’s (Engineering News-Record) latest 2026 survey, which has seen it being ranked among the top two largest general architectural and engineering design firms in the world (among 500 candidates) for two years running.

Recent Earnings Events Have Been Destructive For ACM

Despite boasting such credentials within its industry, it’s fair to say that the investment community hasn’t quite warmed up to ACM for a while now. For instance, over the last 6 months, when ACM’s industrial and mid-cap peers have managed to generate positive returns of double digits, our stock in focus has eroded investor wealth by around -38%. Also, if you scrutinize the image below, you’ll note that much of the weakness over the past six months was triggered by developments in November 2025 and February 2026.

Is there a common denominator in those two months? Yes, indeed, and it essentially boils down to a weak performance by ACM during the earnings season.

Back in the third week of November 2025, after the company released its Q4 earnings (ACM follows a September-ending fiscal), the stock ended up declining for 4 straight days, contracting by -22% in aggregate during that period! The Q1 earnings in February weren't quite as bad, as the stock had initially gapped up following the earnings release the previous day (9th of February), but that initial euphoria proved to be short-lived, as the stock ended up the day lower and saw weakness persist for the next 3 days again, resulting in a -15% drawdown during that period!

Nonetheless, given how inimical earnings season has proven to be for ACM in the recent past, I wouldn’t be surprised to learn if its investors are biting their nails ahead of the next event, which is due in less than two weeks. To be more specific, ACM will release its Q2 2026 earnings on the 11th of May, following the close of the market, with a conference call due to be held the following day before the market opens. So, could Q2 2026 be more rewarding than the recent two quarters? Potentially, yes.

Upcoming Earnings- Main Talking Points

Firstly, investors should be aware that this is a business prone to seasonal shifts across quarters, and Q2 comes after what is traditionally the most challenging quarter for ACM, where harsher weather conditions, a lower number of working days (at least in Q1 2026), and the holiday season schedule ended up hampering the topline and overall productivity. However, do also note that Q2 is certainly not the strongest quarter for ACM, and conditions only meaningfully pick up in the second half of their fiscal year (recall that ACM subscribes to a September year-end). Having said all that, I would still expect ACM’s Q2 2026 topline performance to look good optically, because last year’s base is not bloated (revenue in Q2 2025 had declined by -4% YoY), making it easier to post solid YoY progress. Secondly, consensus expectations ($3.94B) too are on the lower side (having been trimmed by close to 1% over the past month), making it easier for ACM to facilitate a topline beat.

Then Q1 2026 was also keenly impacted by the 43-day government shutdown, a headwind that wasn’t present in Q2 2026, likely resulting in a higher cadence of awards (more so with the passage of all critical federal funding bills), which means even though strong revenue recognition is expected in Q2, one is also likely to see strong bookings, keeping the book-to-burn ratio above 1x, offering solid revenue visibility ahead.

While the private sector side of ACM’s business is not traditionally the largest (29% of the business mix), it has the potential to take on a larger share and drive ACM’s overall growth, given the pace of growth of their data center business, which is growing at a rate of 50% annually. ACM is not only involved in providing services linked to electrical engineering and infrastructure but also due diligence, testing, and feasibility work for hyperscalers.

Q2 will also be the first quarter where the market would have had decisive clarity on ACM’s construction management business, which was previously put up for a strategic review. While the backlog from this business is a plus, what’s also underrated is that it has a strong cash flow profile, which will enable ACM to push through its greater than 100% FCF conversion targets (FCF over net income) through FY29. Speaking of cash, while the real strength will come through in H2 2026, I expect Q2 to be much better than Q1, where the company was unable to generate any progress in free cash flow (YoY) despite a -30% YoY decline in CAPEX (because operating cash flow more than halved).

A Few Reasons To Consider ACM Stock Now

Firstly, it’s worth noting that ACM’s weakness in recent months could also make it more attractive for value-conscious investors. To better explain the valuation backdrop, I’ve also brought into consideration the other major publicly listed professional services and infrastructure consulting firms that operate in the end markets of water, environment, facilities, transportation, public sector, etc. The peer set includes the likes of Jacobs Solutions (J), Stantec (STN), Tetra Tech (TTEK), WSP Global (WSPOF), and Parsons Corp. (PSN).

Note that among all these stocks, ACM is priced at the lowest EBITDA multiple of just 10x, which represents a 15% discount to its peer set and also a 25% discount to the stock’s own rolling average.

I feel the market could be sleeping on ACM’s future potential because it has been fast-tracking AI in its business processes more rapidly than its peers (the benefits of which will be seen by way of faster delivery of design, higher productivity, reduced materials, and overall lower cost of infrastructure) and is poised to see strong operating leverage in the years ahead.

Some naysayers may suggest that ACM’s efforts to drive operating efficiency are just plain fluff, but it’s still worth remembering that this is a management team that has already showcased their ability to extract superior EBITDA from their employees (EBITDA per employee has grown at 1.6x the pace of revenue per employee over the past five years).

Nonetheless, I suspect ACM’s relatively cheap valuations at the current market price will also invigorate ACM’s management team to step up the pace of share repurchases even further. Investors may want to take note of the board-approved authorization that ACM management still has to buy back around $1.34B worth of its stock. This is not a small amount, accounting for 13% of the current market cap, and could help support the share price.

In keeping with the discussion on shareholder returns, also note that ACM is one of those names that tends to be very generous with the dividends it offers. The company has been growing its quarterly dividends every year since 2023, at an impressive CAGR of 20% (in contrast, most construction & engineering stocks don’t even grow their dividends, and of those that do, the growth rate has been much lower at 12-16% over the same duration), and this has also seen it make it to the Nasdaq US Rising Dividend Achievers Index [NURDAI], which covers around 70 US dividend growers that are poised to continue growing their dividends going forward. Investors who want to pursue dividend growers typically pursue the First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers (RDVY) ETF, which tracks NURDAI, or use this ETF as a reference to pursue potential dividend growers. I suspect those who resort to the latter will be interested to note that ACM’s current relative strength ratio versus other dividend growers is at six-year lows and is now trading at a 25% discount to its long-term mean of 1.45x. It is only a matter of time before that ratio mean-reverts to that long-term mean.

Then, if we look at ACM’s long-term monthly charts, we know that it has been in the midst of selling pressure since November 2025, but it appears that things may have gone overboard, and we could see a bottom soon enough. I say this because April’s monthly candle recently hit its lower Bollinger band (a measure of selling overextension, as it is two standard deviations below the 20-month rolling average), while the price is also now closer to levels last seen in 2023, when the stock had bottomed out. Investors may also want to note that the volumes associated with the selling over the past couple of months have been lower than what was seen before.

Conclusion

ACM, which appears to be out of favor, faces a key earnings event in less than two weeks from now. While Q2 earnings may not necessarily be the strongest in the year, we expect better trends from what was seen in Q1, while guidance too could potentially be lifted as the company enters its seasonally stronger H2. After prolonged weakness, we think that ACM may be worth a nibble now, as the stock looks oversold, offers a compelling shareholder return angle (both by way of potentially higher share repurchases as well as strong dividend growth), and is available at dirt-cheap EBITDA valuation multiples, which aren’t necessarily reflecting the expected operating leverage on offer. To conclude, we believe ACM would make for a good buy ahead of its earnings.

data.title: ServiceNow: Buy This Double Bottom - Agentic AI Beneficiary Status Proven
data.description:
I have previously covered Salesforce, Inc. (CRM, CRM:CA) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT, MSFT:CA), discussing their SaaS prospects during the prior SaaSpocalypse meltdown.

NOW Proves Their AI Beneficiary Status

In this article, I shall discuss another Enterprise SaaS player, ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), a company that offers cloud-based AI-powered SaaS offerings, with the recently lowered FY2026 adj gross margin guidance and the delayed closings of several large deals contributing to a steep stock price meltdown of -17.7% (at its worst) post FQ1 '26 earnings call.

This builds upon the prior sector-wide pessimism as well, with NOW already losing -57.2% of its value since the 52-week highs.

1. Updated FY2026 Guidance

For now, let's discuss the recent point of contention - NOW's updated FY2026 guidance.

Given:

  • the recently completed Armis acquisition (cybersecurity),
  • Moveworks acquisition (AI assistant platform),
  • Pyramid Analytics acquisition (AI-powered analytics), and
  • Veza acquisition (AI identity security) along with the intensified integration efforts,

It is unsurprising that the SaaS company expects to suffer near-term subscription adj gross margin headwinds at 81.5% in FY2026 (-2 points YoY/-3 from FY2019 levels of 84.5%).

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the net effect remains positive, as observed in NOW's raised FY2026 subscription revenue guidance of $15.75B at the midpoint (+23.1% YoY), compared to the original guidance of +20.5% YoY offered in the FQ4 '25 earnings call, with it apparent that the acquisitions have been top-line accretive.

NOW's new acquisitions are likely to drive further cross-selling trends upon full integration as well, as the agentic AI boom triggers their consumers' higher workflows and their increased demand for end-to-end stacks across data catalogs, collaboration platforms, LLM models, agentic AI identity governance, and context-aware permission offerings, as similarly reiterated in the recent earnings call:

AI Control Tower also continues to build momentum with average deal sizes more than doubling quarter-over-quarter in Q1. Customers recognize that as AI agents grow in capability, a governed platform to run them isn't optional. It's essential.

(Seeking Alpha.)

This is especially since NOW already expects their "platform leverage" to normalize in 2027 and beyond, with these gross margin headwinds presenting a temporary noise on their performance metrics.

Readers should also note that the "delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East" in Q1 '26 are merely attributed to the ongoing uncertainties from the Iran conflict, with many other companies reporting similar headwinds.

Particularly, readers may want to note that the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising, with NOW expected to report a robust top/bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +19.4%/+19.5% through FY2028.

This is albeit notably decelerating from the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +24.1%/+30.5% and a 10Y CAGR of +29.4%/+46%, respectively, with it perhaps contributing to the stock's ongoing selloff and the consequently discounted valuations against historical levels.

2. FQ1 '26 Numbers Discussed

Otherwise, despite the notably maturing growth profile, I believe that NOW has proven itself as a beneficiary of the ongoing AI boom, as more customers adopt their AI control tower for numerous integrations, cloud applications/tools, cybersecurity/resilience, and AI agent offerings.

Particularly, I am of the opinion that the ongoing AI boom is likely to drive the higher user/agentic AI traffic and the growing "coordinated, autonomous workflows" on its unified platform across different Enterprise use cases (including IT/HR/CRM/security).

This perhaps explains why technological workflows remain NOW's growth driver at 49% of its new Annual Contract Value [ACV] over the LTM, as more Enterprises adopt their AI control tower offerings "to predict and prevent issues, increase productivity, and deliver scalable services."

The same has been observed in their robust FQ1 '26 performance metrics across:

  • the accelerating customer base expansion with higher ACV of $5M+ at 630 customers (+22% YoY), against a year ago at +18.8% YoY,
  • the elevated renewal rate of 97% (-1 point YoY) triggering the higher average ACV at $14.9M per customer (+4.9% YoY) for the $5M+ cohort,
  • the consequently expanding, multi-year Remaining Performance Obligation [RPO] of $27.7B (+25.3% YoY), against a year ago at +24.8% YoY, and
  • The richer adj operating margins of 32% (+1 point YoY/+11 from FY2019 levels of 21%).

These numbers paint an extremely optimistic picture of NOW's profitable and accelerating growth prospects indeed, with the M&A activities already working as intended in enriching its SaaS offerings and entrenching its growing customer base within its AI-powered platform.

Particularly, the management has also tapped into the growing customer demand for consumption-based pricing models:

You'll be happy to know that 50% of net new business now comes from a non-seat-based pricing model, including tokens and other assets such as infrastructure, hardware and connectors.

Our hybrid pricing model gives customers the best of both worlds, predictable foundational seat licenses combined with usage-based scalability. It's the freedom to scale AI adoption without a friction that the customers love.

(Seeking Alpha.)

This development is unsurprising indeed, since hybrid pricing models typically see "faster growth and lower churn compared to peers with no consumption at all," with NOW notably proving the prior market fears of new AI tools potentially disrupting the SaaS sector's per-seat monetization prospects wrong.

Combined with the growing $600B+ in Total Addressable Market [TAM], I believe that the stock's recent meltdown has presented an excellent dip buying opportunity for the contrarian.

3. Profitable Growth Trends Are Compellingly Valued Here

Thanks to the prior SaaS selloff and the market's overreaction to the lowered FY2026 guidance, it is unsurprising that NOW has entered discounted territories at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.88x, down from the recent highs of 74.61x, the 5Y mean of 63.67x, the 10Y mean of 71.39x, and the sector median of 24.06x.

If anything, given the excellent consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +19.5%, I am of the opinion that correction has triggered the cheaper, estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.12x, down from the 5Y mean of 1.99x and the sector median of 1.49x.

When comparing NOW's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.88x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.12x to its enterprise SaaS competitors, including:

  • Workday (WDAY) at 11.39x/0.76x (using the same methodology),
  • Salesforce (CRM) at 13.50x/1.08x,
  • Oracle (ORCL) at 23.22x/1.10x,
  • SAP (SAP) at 20.96x/1.36x, and
  • Microsoft (MSFT) at 25.49x/1.43x, respectively,

it is apparent that NOW is more reasonably valued after the recent correction.

NOW's SaaS outperformance has also been observed in the FQ1 '26 Rule of 56.8% outperformance (+5.2 points YoY), based on the FQ1 '26 revenue growth of +22.4% YoY (against a year ago at +19.5% YoY) and the LTM Free Cash Flow margins of 34.4% (against a year ago at 32.1%).

Based on the management's updated FY2026 guidance, the SaaS company may remain highly profitable at FY2026 Rule of 55.75% outperformance as well (+0.75 points YoY).

Thanks to the rich LTM Free Cash Flow generation of $4.81B (+30.3% sequentially), NOW boasts a healthy balance sheet with a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 0.64x in FQ1 '26 (down from a year ago at 1.13x) and not too far from the Software - Application sector averages of 0.54x.

These reasons are also why the management remains well capitalized to sustain their accretive acquisition trends, while cancelling the elevated LTM SBC expenses of $2.03B (+13.4% sequentially) through the ongoing share repurchases to deliver the stable share count at 1.04B in FQ1'26 (inline YoY).

NOW Is Initiated As A Strong Buy Here

For now, NOW has seemingly double bottomed at the $83/$84s in April 2026, with it presenting investors an excellent entry point after the stock lost nearly all of their 2022/2021 and 2023/2024 gains by the time of writing.

Given the steep correction, it unsurprising that NOW has entered oversold territories by the time of writing, with the potential bottoming at current levels implying an excellent dip buying opportunity.

This is especially since the stock is now trading near to my base-case fair value estimates of $80.30, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.88x and the LTM adj EPS performance of $3.67 (+26.1% sequentially).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $6.06, there remains an excellent upside potential of +46.6% to my base-case long-term price target of $132.60 as well.

This is not even taking into account NOW's potential valuation upgrading nearer to the 1Y P/E mean of 38.65x (not too far from the peer group 5Y P/E mean of 37x), with it likely to trigger a more than doubling upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $234.20, thanks to the overly done SaaSpocalypse fears.

As a result of the excellent risk/reward at the current discounted P/E levels, the potential emergence of a double bottom at the $80s, the likely to be resilient, profitable growth trends from the growing RPOs/expanding customer base, and the likely to remain stable shareholder equity from the ongoing share repurchases, I am initiating a Strong Buy rating for the NOW stock here.

data.title: Coca-Cola Q1: Why 12% Growth Is A Calendar Illusion
data.description:
It’s been a while since I covered The Coca-Cola Company (KO). The last time was in August 2025 (check it here), and since then the stock’s total return has been 9%. As my rating was a Hold and I had concerns, even though this total return has not been completely out of the ordinary, its performance surprised me.

Basically, in this article, I argued that although I recognize and believe in the thesis that Coca-Cola is that type of high-quality compounder, I have concerns regarding valuation vs. the growth the company actually delivers.

But the Q1 earnings impressed me positively. We are talking about a 10% top-line growth and operating income growing even more, showing that Coca-Cola can still improve operating leverage. But even so, I don’t think it is worth an upgrade to Buy, also due to the quarter’s yellow flags.

KO's Q1 Earnings: Pretty Solid

Although I don’t find the way KO reports earnings very visually appealing, I really like that in 2 tables it is possible to understand the result almost completely (and you can already notice why it was a double beat).

Note below that organic revenues were 10% YoY, which is due to an improvement in concentrate sales (8% growth) and also a 2% increase in price/mix. Basically, it is a very strong top line. There was a price increase/higher penetration of premium products, which is positive and shows pricing power but also shows that Coca-Cola’s volume isn’t dead even though it is already very mature.

Another positive point to note in this top-line growth is that the currency impact was, unlike other quarters, very positive, so the “GAAP” top-line growth was 12%, an extremely solid number for Coca-Cola’s profile.

In the breakdown I also found it very healthy. There was no dependence on any specific region; all performed well in the KPI of concentrate sales, with highlights for North America with 11%, driven by the company’s strategies to mitigate headwinds and ride the new momentum of the industry, such as smaller cans, premium products, zero coke, etc.

The negative highlight is Asia Pacific, not only in revenue but also in operating income. Although the region grew well, partly due to marketing initiatives, there was a relevant decline in price, and because of these higher expenses/costs, the region’s operating income declined 14%. The good part is that this was not enough to affect Coca-Cola’s overall operating income, which kept growing ~12% (non-GAAP), above organic revenue (non-GAAP), showing that revenue is growing more than expenses such as SG&A. More specifically, while reported revenue was 12% YoY, SG&A grew 7%, that is, considerable operating leverage, especially considering KO’s maturity.

Up to this point, everything is great, right? Surprising. Coca-Cola grew its top line 12%, operating income 19%, and EPS ~18%.

But I have to mention that the quarter is not quite as positive as it seems. Not only because of some issues like Asia Pacific, but mainly because YoY, this quarter had 6 more days. Having 6 more days means there is a ~6.5% tailwind in the top line (considering a 90-day quarter). 6.5% more days to sell, 6.5% more days for people to consume, so it makes sense for top-line growth to look so positive at first glance. But looking annually, this will have a neutral effect; the company itself has already indicated that in Q4 2026 there will be 6 fewer days YoY, so the result will come “worse.”

But even so, it is not possible to treat this result as bad; it is still very solid. Let’s say that without the ~6% tailwind from additional days, top-line growth would have grown something between 5% and 6%. It is a good difference, right? But it is still a very solid number. As a reference, revenue in the last 10 years had a CAGR of ~0.8%; therefore, growing the top line in mid-single digits while growing operating income above that is already very positive.

KO's Valuation: Is It Enough?

The first point to understand whether KO’s valuation (~23x forward earnings) is good enough or not is to see what the reasonable assumptions are. Basically, we need to know what to expect from this company in the next 5, 10, and 20 years.

As I mentioned, the ~12% top-line growth in this quarter is an outlier; we cannot assume that in the coming years Coca-Cola Company will be able to continue growing at this pace, not only because of the 6 extra days but also due to other factors such as the maturity of this industry, headwinds in sugar beverage consumption, and currency impacts.

The market estimates growth until 2030 of around ~4%, being 2% in 2027. I partially agree, but I think that given the current quarter, we can have slightly better expectations, as Coca-Cola is proving to be a company that knows how to ride this environment.

But in any case, my assumptions will be ~3% in 2027, 5% until 2030, and then falling to 3%, slightly above consensus.

For net income margin, I will assume something close to 28%, but reaching 29% through operating leverage, more automation, maturation of new ventures, efficiency initiatives, and other impacts that technology (such as AI) may have in the coming years.

Having the net income flow, we now have the P/E, which reaches ~18x in 2032, which no longer seems so attractive.

But the main point here will be the assumptions of terminal growth and discount rate. For terminal growth, I will be a bit more optimistic and say that after 2032, Coca-Cola Company will be able to grow 3% per year. Basically, a return slightly above inflation, and I believe it can deliver this mainly through margin expansion and eventual price increases above inflation (and not from volumes).

In this scenario, if our discount rate is 8%, the model points to a fair market cap of $319 billion, very similar to the current $324 billion. So basically, we have a reverse model. If this scenario happens, and it seems to me to be closer to a base/reasonable scenario, without being too pessimistic or too optimistic, Coca-Cola stock’s internal rate of return (IRR) is 8%.

Particularly, even though 8% is a minimally interesting compounding pace for a wide-moat company like KO, I think it is low. I don’t think it is good enough. Maybe you think I was too pessimistic, so let’s say terminal growth is 4%, because KO will still have a lot of growth exploring new markets in the coming decades. In this scenario, the IRR would still be 8.7%.

Here there is a somewhat personal aspect; maybe for a conservative investor who wants stability in cash flow, ~8% return per year is enough to have KO in the portfolio. In my view, it is not. Especially because I don’t think it is a completely stable case, of course it is a solid company, but there are risks of competition, changes in consumer preference, besides other uncertainties that eventually appear for any company when we are talking about decades, such as some type of lawsuit, fine, etc.

Bottom Line

I believe that even after KO’s solid Q1 earnings, the most appropriate rating here is a hold. It is a good company that will still be able to deliver decent shareholder value, but I don’t find that attractive enough to be optimistic, and I also don’t think it is so bad as to say that KO stock does not fit any portfolio and should be avoided at all costs.

Therefore, I reiterate my Hold rating for KO stock.
